narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 76% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows you ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your LSAT score of 31 and undergraduate GPA of 2.7 are also noticeably lower than the averages of 36.7 and 3.21 seen across applicants who tend to pass the bar. Coming from an upper-middle-income family puts you in a solid but not exceptional bracket compared to peers, while being a white male pursuing full-time studies aligns closely with the majority of students in the dataset. The model weighed these factors to predict your bar outcome, with your first-year grade decile having the strongest pull toward a failure prediction—likely because it signals early academic struggles that often persist. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which pushed the prediction further toward failure due to its below-average mark. Your third-year decile ranking came next in influence, reinforcing concerns about inconsistent performance over time. Family income level followed, having a moderate but still unfavorable nudge, then your undergraduate GPA added to the risk signal. Less influential but still tilting against you were your racial background and gender, which matched common profiles without providing a boost, and your full-time status, typical for most passers but not a standout here. Overall, your metrics fell short of those typical successful students, stacking up to tip the scales toward rejection. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher than average, build stronger study habits to overcome those early low deciles, and seek ways to demonstrate academic turnaround, like extra coursework or prep programs proven to lift bar passage rates. With targeted effort on these academic predictors, you could shift your profile toward the passing side many students achieve. Hang in there—many have rebounded from similar starts.
